title: Share of artificial intelligence jobs among all job postings
subtitle: A job posting is considered an AI job if it requests one or more AI skills, e.g., "natural language processing", "neural networks", "machine learning", or "robotics".
caption:
Share of artificial intelligence jobs among all job postings
A job posting is considered an AI job if it requests one or more AI skills, e.g., "natural language processing", "neural networks", "machine learning", or "robotics".

Across nine countries between 2014 and 2024, the share of job postings requesting artificial intelligence skills varies by nation and time, measured as a percentage of total postings. The vertical axis ranges from 0% to 1.8%, with horizontal gridlines every 0.2 percentage points. The United States line, colored brown, begins near 0.6% in 2014, rises steadily to 1.4% by 2018, dips slightly in 2020, then climbs to 1.7% in 2022 before falling to 1.4% in 2023 and rising again to 1.8% in 2024 — the highest value shown on the chart. The United Kingdom, in orange, starts at 0.4% in 2014, reaches 0.9% by 2018, peaks near 1.35% in 2022, drops to 0.95% in 2023, and recovers to 1.25% in 2024. Sweden’s purple line begins around 0.9% in 2019, rises to 1.25% in 2021, falls to 1.05% in 2022, then climbs to 1.3% in 2024. Spain, in dark blue, starts at 0.35% in 2019, jumps to 1.15% in 2021, dips to 1.0% in 2023, and ends near 1.25% in 2024. Austria’s green line begins at 0.6% in 2019, rises to 1.35% in 2022, then settles at 1.05% in 2024. Italy’s lavender line starts at 0.5% in 2019, fluctuates between 0.65% and 0.9%, ending at 0.85% in 2024. New Zealand, in light blue, remains below 0.6% throughout, starting at 0.15% in 2014 and reaching 0.55% in 2024. The Netherlands’ teal line appears only in 2024 at 1.2%. The United States consistently holds the highest share across all years where data is available, while New Zealand remains the lowest throughout the period.
